description: The provided image displays a "Check for updates" system icon and contains no discernible scientific content; all micrographic, spectroscopic, or plot details are unreadable from the current pixels. Based on the accompanying paper context for low-density FeMnAlC steels deciphered via machine learning, the intended figure likely supports the composition–microstructure correlation study of the validated Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C alloy. The text reports experimental characterization encompassing X-ray diffraction for FCC and κ-phase identification, scanning electron microscopy and electron probe microanalysis for homogeneous elemental distribution, transmission electron microscopy with selected-area electron diffraction for nanoscale intragranular κ-carbide precipitates, and electron backscatter diffraction confirming a recrystallized single-phase austenite matrix with an average grain size of 17 μm. Mechanical property data indicate a yield strength of 953 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 1109 MPa, and total elongation of 42%. Furthermore, the machine learning workflow—employing KNN, ANN, CART, SVM, and random forest models—may be visualized. Because the scientific image is not visible, specific sub-panel identities, legends, peak positions, and microstructural features cannot be directly confirmed.

Figure 1 presents a machine-learning-driven design framework for low-density Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic steels. The workflow begins with a large pool of possible candidates and applies a sequential filter supported by predicting models based on machine-learning methods. The screening criteria are: (1) existence of κ-carbide; (2) a single γ-austenite region; (3) a (γ+κ) dual-phase region within 500–600 °C; (4) high κ-phase fraction; and (5) low density. Candidates passing all five diamond-shaped decision nodes yield an empirical formula, which then proceeds to experimental tests. If the tested properties are unsatisfactory, a feedback loop returns the results to refine the predicting models; if good properties are confirmed, the design succeeds. This framework establishes the composition–microstructure correlation and enables high-throughput down-selection from thousands of candidates to optimized steel compositions, ultimately validated on Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C.

This figure displays a Pearson correlation coefficient matrix for ten original descriptors utilized in machine-learning modeling of low-density FeMnAlC steels. The triangular heatmap employs colored circles whose size and saturation represent correlation strength, with blue indicating positive and red indicating negative correlations. The descriptors comprise elemental compositions (Mn, Al, C, Fe), physical parameters (VEC, Tm), and thermodynamic or geometric features (ΔSmix, ΔHmix, Δχ, δ). Visible matrix values reveal strong positive correlations such as C–Δχ (0.97), Δχ–δ (0.98), and δ–C (0.98), alongside strong negative correlations including Al–VEC (-0.94), C–ΔHmix (-0.93), and Δχ–ΔHmix (-0.93). As stated in the caption and citation, the authors applied a Pearson filter to remove redundancy, defining highly correlated features as those with coefficients exceeding 0.95; consequently, Δχ and δ were eliminated from the feature pool. This feature-selection step retained non-redundant inputs to enhance model robustness for deciphering composition–microstructure relationships in the studied steel system.

Figure 3 presents the machine learning algorithm selection and relative importance of eight compositional descriptors for predicting microstructural regimes in low-density FeMnAlC steels. Panel (a) compares the 10-fold cross-validation accuracy of five algorithms—KNN, ANN, SVM, RF, and CART—for three classifications: the existence of the κ-phase (Y₁), a γ+κ dual-phase region at 500–600°C (Y₂), and a single γ region (Y₃). Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy across all targets, whereas ANN performed poorly for Y₃. Panels (b–d) show the mean decrease in accuracy when each descriptor is removed. For the single γ region (Y₃, panel b) and κ-phase existence (Y₁, panel d), Al exhibits the greatest importance, while C shows the weakest effect. For the γ+κ dual-phase region at 500–600°C (Y₂, panel c), Mn and Fe are the most influential descriptors, with C again having the least impact. These results establish RF as the optimal classifier and reveal that Al governs κ-phase precipitation and austenite stability, whereas Mn and Fe dominate the dual-phase regime.

Figure 4 presents machine-learning regression results for predicting the high κ-phase fraction in low-density FeMnAlC steels. Panel (a) compares five regression algorithms—KNN, ANN, SVM, RF, and CART—via 10-fold cross-validation RMSE (%). SVM exhibits the lowest RMSE (caption states <1 %), outperforming ANN (~1.2 %), KNN (~1.3 %), RF (~1.7 %), and CART (~2.4 %), establishing SVM as the optimal regressor for this dataset. Panel (b) assesses the relative importance of eight descriptors (Mn, Al, C, Fe, VEC, Tm, ΔSmix, ΔHmix) by measuring the increase in cross-validation RMSE upon removal of each individual descriptor. Removal of Al produces the largest RMSE rise (to ~1.25 %), followed by VEC (~1.15 %), indicating that Al is the most influential compositional factor for predicting κ-phase fraction, whereas removing Mn, C, Fe, Tm, ΔSmix, or ΔHmix results in comparatively minor perturbations around ~1.0–1.05 %. Panel (c) displays the predicted versus true κ-phase fraction from the SVM model under 10-fold cross-validation, with data points tightly clustered along the parity line and a correlation coefficient R = 0.99. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that an SVM regressor achieves high-precision prediction of κ-carbide volume fraction in FeMnAlC alloys, with Al content identified as the dominant descriptor.

This figure presents a two-step machine-learning-guided screening workflow (Fig. 5) for optimizing Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steels, beginning with 78,477 initial compositional candidates (Area A). The first screening stage enforces phase-component boundary conditions—specifically κ-phase existence, a single γ-austenite region, and a stable (γ+κ) dual-phase region between 500°C and 600°C—reducing the candidate pool to 1,320 (Area B). The second stage applies performance-based constraints of κ-phase fraction ≥ 10% and density ≤ 7 g/cm³, further narrowing the space to 150 final candidates (Area C). The schematic employs progressively sparser dot arrays within circles to visually encode the drastic reduction of the search space. This workflow demonstrates that data-driven screening can efficiently isolate promising low-density steel compositions, with the resulting 150 candidates forming the targeted search space subsequently validated experimentally on Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C.

This figure presents a five-panel composition–phase screening map for FeMnAlC austenitic low-density steel at a fixed 30 wt% Mn, plotting Al (1–12 wt%) versus C (0–2.2 wt%) to guide structural optimization via machine learning. Panel (a) marks the κ-phase existence domain (Y1), showing κ-phase precipitation is primarily triggered when Al exceeds ~5 wt%. Panel (b) identifies the γ+κ dual-phase region at 500–600 °C (Y2), localized roughly at 4.5–9.5 wt% Al and 0.4–1.1 wt% C. Panel (c) delineates the single γ-phase region (Y3), which expands to higher Al levels with increasing C. Panel (d) highlights the intersection of Y1 and Y3 in red, defining a composition window where a single austenite matrix can host κ-phase precipitates. Panel (e) further narrows this to the triple intersection (Y1+Y2+Y3), pinpointing the optimal candidate space (approximately 5.5–8.5 wt% Al and 0.4–1.1 wt% C) that satisfies single-phase austenite with γ+κ dual-phase stability upon aging. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that Al governs κ-phase formation and austenite stability, enabling multi-criteria compositional design.

Figure 7 presents the second-step performance screening of low-density FeMnAlC steels by machine learning, displaying composition-dependent κ-phase volume fraction and density maps together with the optimization workflow. Panel (a) is a contour map plotting κ fraction (%) versus Al (5.0–9.0 wt.%) and C (0.4–1.1 wt.%), indicating that κ fraction rises toward higher Al and C contents and exceeds 11.00% in the upper-right region. Panel (c) shows the corresponding density map (g/cm³), where density decreases with increasing Al, reaching below 6.900 g/cm³ in the high-Al domain. Panel (b) depicts the screening protocol in Al-C space: blue circles denote candidates meeting the κ-fraction target, green circles denote the density target, overlapped target components are shown as darker dots, and the red star marks the validation composition Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C. By applying the tightened constraints κ fraction ≥ 11.000% and density ≤ 6.900 g/cm³, 30 candidate compositions were identified, leading to the selection of Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C for experimental corroboration.

Figure 8 assembles multi-scale microstructural evidence for Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel following aging at 550 °C (as reported in the text). (a) The XRD profile exhibits exclusively FCC austenite reflections indexed as γ(111), γ(200), γ(220), γ(311), and γ(222), indicating a single γ-phase matrix. (b) The EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) map displays a fully recrystallized grain structure (scale bar 35 μm), and the caption states an average grain size of 17 μm; (c) the EBSD phase map (scale bar 35 μm) shows a predominant FCC phase (red) with little to no BCC phase (green). (d) Bright-field and (e) dark-field TEM images (scale bar 100 nm) reportedly reveal nanoscale κ-carbide precipitates, with the dark-field micrograph highlighting them in bright contrast. (f) The selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern along the Z=[011̄] zone axis shows FCC matrix spots together with additional weak reflections linked to the κ phase. Collectively, these observations confirm that the steel possesses a recrystallized single FCC austenitic matrix containing coherent nanoscale intragranular κ-carbide precipitates formed during 550 °C aging, which are critical for precipitation strengthening.

Figure 9 presents experimental validation of the Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel processed at 1000°C/15 min and aged at 550°C/2 h. Panel (a) shows element distribution maps for Fe, Mn, Al, and C alongside a secondary electron image (SEI) and a backscattered electron composition image (COMPO), all at a 20 μm scale; the uniformly colored elemental maps visually indicate homogeneous elemental distribution without significant segregation, supporting the formation of a single-phase austenitic matrix. Panel (b) displays the engineering stress–strain curve for the same alloy; caption reports a yield strength of 953 MPa, an ultimate tensile strength of 1109 MPa, and an elongation of 42%. Panel (c) provides an optical micrograph at a 50 μm scale showing a fully recrystallized, equiaxed grain structure. Collectively, these results confirm that the machine-learning-designed composition achieves the predicted uniform single-phase microstructure coupled with excellent strength–ductility synergy.
